statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given a prime number $ p $ , $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ , and an integer $ k $ .

Find the number of pairs of indexes $ (i, j) $ ( $ 1 \le i < j \le n $ ) for which $ (a_i \oplus a_j)(a_i^2 \oplus a_j^2) \equiv k \bmod p $ .

Here $ \oplus $ denotes the [bitwise XOR operation](https://word.rodeo/?utm_source=share#cvMcraT7fZ6Yj3T3fwWbp8jppto8Pan9HXzC5fWYjPCCvqUMqseEkRFkzttufIM=).

Input Format
The first line contains integers $ n, p, k $ ( $ 2 \le n \le 3 \cdot 10^5 $ , $ 2 \le p \le 10^9 $ , $ 0 \le k \le p-1 $ ). $ p $ is guaranteed to be prime.

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ ( $ 0 \le a_i \le p-1 $ ). It is guaranteed that all elements are different.

Output Format
Output a single integer — answer to the problem.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3 3 2
0 1 2
Output
1

Sample 2
Input
6 11 2
1 3 5 6 7 8
Output
3

Note
In the first example:

$ (0\oplus1)(0^2 \oplus 1^2) = 1 \equiv 1 \bmod 3 $ .

$ (0\oplus2)(0^2 \oplus 2^2) = 8 \equiv 2 \bmod 3 $ .

$ (1\oplus2)(1^2 \oplus 2^2) = 15 \equiv 0 \bmod 3 $ .

So only $ 1 $ pair satisfies the condition.

In the second example, there are $ 3 $ such pairs: $ (1, 5) $ , $ (1, 6) $ , $ (3, 6) $ .

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
If you click on the link to the
bitwise XOR operation
, you will actually be taken to a Wordle. Solving it will give you the answer $$$\texttt{MULTIPLY}$$$ (Since you can submit non-English words, and you have infinite tries (The page actually only allows 6 tries, but you can get around this by revisiting the page in incognito mode), the solution is actually relatively straightforward — you can find all the letters and where they appear by submitting strings each consisting of the same letter, then just combine all the guesses.). Thus $$$\oplus$$$ actually denotes the multiply operation.
So the solution is actually to find the number pairs of indexes $$$(i, j)$$$ for which $$$a_i^3 a_j^3 \equiv k \bmod p$$$. We can do this in $$$\mathcal{O}(n \log n + n \log p)$$$ time if we go from left to right and, for each $$$j$$$, count the number of $$$i$$$ such that $$$a_i^3 \equiv k a_j^{-3} \bmod p$$$ by keeping a map storing how frequent each $$$a_j^3$$$ was so far.
Note that there is an edge case when $$$k = 0$$$ which you should handle separately.
